point_001: The primary baseline (GENRE) uses a different pre-trained model (BART-large) than the proposed method (T5), making direct performance comparisons potentially unfair; a reproduced GENRE under identical conditions is needed.
point_002: The experimental setup is incomplete: important baselines such as BM25, DPR, and SEAL are not evaluated under the same comparable settings on the main benchmarks.
point_003: The paper does not train on the full KILT dataset, limiting the scope and generalizability of the experimental conclusions.
point_004: The performance of GENRE, DSI, and SEAL on NQ-320K appears surprisingly low and is not adequately explained or contextualized.
point_005: The technical novelty is considered limited, as applying contextualized token embeddings in a retrieval decoder is conceptually similar to existing approaches like KNN-LMs.
point_006: The model description and Figure 1 are insufficiently clear; it is difficult to fully understand the architecture (e.g., what text is fed into the encoder for contextual embeddings, what 'Cape(1)' and 'Cape(2)' represent).
point_007: The paper lacks a formal or complete mathematical description of the model, relying only on Figure 1 and a brief verbal description in Section 3.
point_008: Evaluation is restricted to title-based or document-ID retrieval datasets and does not cover large-scale document retrieval collections where fine-grained content discrimination is critical.
point_009: It is unclear how the model handles unseen named entities, novel events, or documents not present during training, which is a fundamental concern for dynamic document collections.
point_010: The impact of design choices in building the contextual representations (e.g., clustering strategy, alternative embedding technologies) is not analyzed or ablated.
point_011: The paper does not discuss limitations of the proposed approach, nor does it situate the work relative to classical generative retrieval models (e.g., bag-of-words or n-gram language models).
